ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Beijing-style spicy sizzling lamb specifically. FOODON_03311349 (lamb dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (lamb and mutton meat, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for spiced iron-plate lamb preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227197003 (lamb meat, food) is the closest available concept. No specific concept for Beijing-style spicy sizzling lamb exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Beijing-style spicy sizzling lamb is approximately 300 g, typically presented on a hot iron plate as a main dish for one person.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, composite of retail cuts, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17004); adapted with spice and oil additions estimated from USDA Foundation Foods cumin and chili data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base lamb values derived from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked composite lamb cuts (NDB 17004, approximately 258 kcal/100 g cooked). Adjustments applied for added cooking oil (~5 g/100 g dish), chili, cumin, soy sauce, and garlic as minor contributors. Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce seasoning typical of this preparation style. Final per-100 g values represent a reasonable estimate for a spiced, oil-cooked lamb sizzling dish. Confidence is medium due to absence of a direct laboratory analysis for this specific restaurant dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; analogous estimate based on cooked lamb and spiced meat dish category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beijing-style spicy sizzling lamb. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database category for cooked lamb and spiced meat dishes, which are predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate. Carbohydrate content is low (~3.2 g/100 g), primarily from minor starch in marinade and cumin. GL per 300 g serving = (15 × 9.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.44, rounded to 1.4. This is consistent with low-GI classification for high-protein, high-fat meat dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce seasoning (~480 mg/100 g; ~1440 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less and request reduced soy sauce seasoning, as a full 300 g serving provides approximately 1440 mg sodium — nearly 60% of the recommended daily limit of 2400 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~3.2 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~15) with negligible glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beijing-style spicy sizzling lamb is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index; however, the high fat content warrants attention to total caloric intake and saturated fat limits.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~5.1 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~72 mg/100 g) from lamb, combined with elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions (≤150 g), as a full serving contributes approximately 15 g saturated fat and 216 mg cholesterol alongside high sodium, all of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beijing Style Spicy Sizzling Lamb (京城香辣铁板羊, Jīng Chéng Xiāng Là Tiě Bǎn Yáng) is a Chinese restaurant dish originating from Beijing cuisine traditions, popularised across Chinese-style restaurants in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish features thinly sliced or cubed lamb that is marinated in a spiced mixture of chili, cumin powder, garlic, ginger, and soy sauce, then cooked at high heat on a cast-iron sizzling plate (铁板, tiě bǎn). The result is lamb that is crispy and caramelised on the exterior while remaining tender and juicy inside, with a bold, aromatic flavour profile dominated by cumin and chili heat. Cumin (孜然, zīrán) is a hallmark spice of northern Chinese lamb cookery, particularly associated with Xinjiang and Beijing street food traditions. The sizzling plate presentation adds theatrical appeal and keeps the dish hot throughout the meal. Nutritionally, this dish is high in protein (~18.5 g/100 g) and moderate-to-high in fat (~14.2 g/100 g), with very low carbohydrate content (~3.2 g/100 g), making it a low-glycemic-index food (GI ~15). The sodium content is elevated (~480 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce seasoning, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The dish provides meaningful amounts of zinc, iron, vitamin B12, and niacin from the lamb. It is suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns and individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, but should be consumed with caution by those managing cardiovascular disease or hypertension due to its saturated fat and sodium content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Beijing lamb dish?

confidence_reason: No direct laboratory analysis or approved primary database entry exists for this specific Beijing-style spicy sizzling lamb restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked composite lamb data (NDB 17004) with adjustments for added cooking oil, soy sauce sodium, and spice contributions. The adaptation is methodologically sound but introduces estimation uncertainty. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for cooked spiced meat dishes. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb (NDB 17004) with adaptations for oil, soy sauce, and spice additions; no direct laboratory analysis is available for this specific dish. 2. Sodium value (~480 mg/100 g) reflects soy sauce seasoning typical of this preparation; actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy lamb data and are not independently verified for this specific dish. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The dish is served at See Ya Tonight (今晚有局), Singapore, at SGD 38.74 per portion. 6. Halal status is assumed based on lamb being a halal-permissible meat; actual halal certification of the restaurant has not been verified. 7. Gluten is potentially present from soy sauce (wheat-containing); individuals with coeliac disease should verify tamari or gluten-free soy sauce use. 8. All -1 values in per_100g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy lamb data and represent best available approximations rather than confirmed absent values; they are populated with numeric estimates rather than -1 because source data supports reasonable estimation.
